topic: A person finds out their surprise party has been cancelled
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox with the stark finality of a closing door. "Please note that due to unforeseen circumstances, the surprise celebration planned for Saturday has been cancelled." I reread the words three times, my thumb hovering over the screen, a strange, cold knot tightening in my chest. The vibrant anticipation that had been humming beneath my skin for weeks suddenly sputtered out, replaced by a hollow, echoing silence. I stood up abruptly, pacing the length of my living room, my footsteps loud against the hardwood floor. My hands started to tremble slightly, and I pressed the heels of my palms against my eyes, trying to force the image of the planned gathering—the laughter, the carefully curated decorations—back into focus, but it kept dissolving into a frustrating, blurry mess. A deep, unpleasant tightness settled behind my ribs, making it difficult to draw a full breath.

[story 2]

I stared at the text message, the screen illuminating the faint lines of worry etched around my eyes. *Cancelled*. The single word felt monumental, a physical weight pressing down on my shoulders. My mouth went dry, and I felt a sudden, dizzying lurch in my stomach. I walked over to the window, pulling the curtains aside just enough to look out at the ordinary street, as if seeking some external validation that things were still normal. My breath hitched, shallow and quick. I turned back to the computer, my fingers hovering over the keyboard, unable to type, unable to process the abrupt deflation of hope. A low, involuntary sound escaped my throat—a small, choked noise—and I quickly clamped my jaw shut, trying to regain some semblance of composure, but my hands kept flexing, restless and unstill.

[story 3]

When I finally read the notification, the air in the room seemed to thin out. It wasn't a sudden shock, more like a slow, creeping numbness that started at my fingertips and radiated inward. I let the phone slip from my grasp; it hit the rug with a soft thud, the sound seeming disproportionately loud in the quiet apartment. I sank onto the sofa, the cushions seeming to swallow me whole. I didn't move for several minutes, just sitting there, staring blankly at the wall where the confetti had been supposed to be. A strange, hot prickling sensation began on my neck, a tension that coiled tight in my shoulders. When I finally managed to push myself up, my movements were slow, deliberate, as if moving through thick water. My voice, when I finally spoke, came out surprisingly thin and reedy.
